followup_notes.changes_from_prior_cycle: I keep the same overall verdict but slightly lower the overall score from 6.4 to 6.1 after crediting other reviewers' point about the unstated infinite-time switching/convergence assumption and the dependence on external mode formulas. I do not adopt the strongest positive review's view that the derivation is fully rigorous or free of missing assumptions.
invalid_points_from_other_reviewers: I do not accept the claim that the paper provides a rigorous alternative derivation without caveats; several analytic and normalization assumptions remain implicit.
I treat the missing spacetime dimension in the HTML as mainly a rendering/bundle issue rather than a substantive paper flaw unless it is also absent in the PDF.

I would not downgrade merely because Eq. (5)'s thermal coefficients are not fully derived; this is a standard result and the reply cites Birrell-Davies, though the continuum formalism should be interpreted carefully.
The suggested need to prove massive Rindler-mode completeness is at most a minor expository issue for this genre, not a central objection.
valid_points_from_other_reviewers: The leading contribution at order lambda^2 is correctly identified; this reinforces the perturbative structure in Section V.
The exact comparison with the 2015 formula cannot be fully checked from the Reply alone because the explicit mode functions and normalizations are imported from [1].
The all-time Rindler evolution should mention adiabatic switching, a regulator, or a convergence prescription.
The causality objection based solely on support of intermediate basis modes is weak; global support of basis functions does not by itself imply acausal dynamics.

main_claims.claim: The right Rindler wedge can be treated as a globally hyperbolic spacetime with constant Rindler-time Cauchy slices for evolving the coupled system.
main_claims.notes: This is standard and the use made of it is limited. Other reviewers' agreement on this point increases my confidence.
main_claims.paper_location: Sections I and IV, especially the paragraph before Eq. (6)

main_claims.claim: The ambient field state induced in the right wedge by the Minkowski vacuum is the thermal Rindler state written in Eq. (5).
main_claims.notes: The physical statement is standard. I retain the caveat that Eq. (5) is formal for continuum modes and should be interpreted with a regulator, wave packets, or algebraic KMS-state language rather than as an ordinary trace-class density matrix over an infinite continuum tensor product.

main_claims.claim: The leading de-excitation probability comes from the U1 rho_i U1^dagger term and gives Eq. (11), with coefficients defined in Eqs. (12)-(13).
main_claims.notes: The perturbative selection rules and thermal-trace structure look plausible. The exact numerical equality depends on mode normalizations and the interaction Hamiltonian convention, which are not fully self-contained in the Reply.

main_claims.claim: Eq. (11) agrees with Eq. (19) of Lorek-Louko-Dragan 2015, so the prior formula is not invalid merely because the earlier derivation used both Rindler wedges in intermediate mode expansions.
main_claims.notes: The wedge-only derivation addresses the main stated objection in kind. Exact agreement with the 2015 notation still requires a human comparison of the original mode functions, normalizations, and gamma definitions.
main_claims.paper_location: Section V after Eq. (13), and Section VI

main_claims.claim: Using basis modes with support outside the interacting cavity region does not by itself violate causality.
main_claims.notes: I agree with the other reviewers that the plane-wave analogy is a valid high-level rebuttal. The paper's version remains heuristic rather than a detailed Tomonaga-Schwinger or microcausality proof, but it is adequate for a short Reply.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The cavity endpoints obey the inequality needed to keep the whole cavity in the right Rindler wedge.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: Without this, the geometric setup and Dirichlet cavity mode expansion are not well-defined as stated.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Eq. (5) is a formal representation of a KMS/quasi-free state, not a literal trace-class density matrix over continuum modes.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Section III Eq. (5), Section V traces
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: This is needed for a mathematically controlled interpretation of the thermal sums and traces.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The interaction Hamiltonian in Eq. (6) is exactly the intended model Hamiltonian and is normalized consistently with the 2015 paper.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: A different lapse or measure convention would alter the gamma overlap coefficients and could break the claimed agreement.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The all-time perturbative integrals are regulated or convergent in the same sense as in the original calculation.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Section IV Eqs. (7)-(8), Section V Eq. (11)
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: Without a switching or convergence prescription, transition probabilities in continuum QFT can be distributional or divergent.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The omitted explicit mode functions from [1] have the normalizations required for Eqs. (12)-(13) to reproduce Eq. (19) of [1].
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The central reply rests on exact agreement with the earlier formula, not just structural similarity.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Does Eq. (11) exactly match Eq. (19) of Lorek-Louko-Dragan 2015 under the same mode normalizations and gamma conventions?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The Reply imports the explicit mode functions and normalizations from [1]; verifying equality requires detailed comparison with the original formulas.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Is Eq. (6) precisely the interaction Hamiltonian of the original model, with no missing lapse, metric determinant, or coupling rescaling?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: This depends on the model definition in [1] and on whether the interaction is phenomenological Hamiltonian input or derived from a covariant action.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Are the Omega integrals and infinite-time limits in Eq. (11) well-defined after the intended switching/regularization, including behavior near Omega=0?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: This requires asymptotic analysis of the specific Rindler modes and the finite/infinite time overlap integrals.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Does Toussaint's Comment raise objections beyond the two-wedge mode issue, and if so are they addressed?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The review bundle summarizes only the objection as described in the Reply; assessing completeness requires reading the Comment itself.

novelty_assessment: Novelty remains limited. This is a short Reply whose contribution is not new mathematics or new QFT machinery, but a cleaner wedge-local derivation addressing a specific methodological criticism. I give it somewhat more credit for significance than one reviewer did because resolving a published objection can matter, but the contribution is still routine and corrective rather than field-shaping.
score_rationale: The paper appears to answer the specific two-wedge objection at the level expected of a short physics Reply, and the causality rebuttal is substantially right. I lower technical soundness for unresolved formal issues: continuum thermal traces, interaction normalization, all-time switching/convergence, and reliance on [1] for exact formula matching. Novelty is low by genre; significance is moderate because the clarification protects a published result but has limited broader reach.
summary: The Reply gives a narrow wedge-local rederivation of the de-excitation probability formula challenged by Toussaint, replacing the original full-Minkowski/two-Rindler-wedge intermediate calculation with evolution entirely in the right Rindler wedge and the Minkowski-vacuum restricted thermal state. I still view the central argument as plausible and useful for the limited purpose of the reply, but not as a fully rigorous standalone proof: the continuum thermal state, interaction normalization, convergence/switching, and exact comparison to the 2015 formula remain mostly delegated to standard physics conventions or to the earlier paper.

technical_objections.evidence: The text states a cavity of proper length l at endpoints built from alpha^{-1} +/- l/2 but does not state the inequality ensuring the left endpoint is not at or beyond the Rindler horizon.
technical_objections.objection: The allowed cavity-length range is not explicitly stated. The endpoint definitions require both walls to remain inside the right Rindler wedge, in particular the lower endpoint must be positive, which in the notation of the prior review gives l < 2/alpha.
technical_objections.paper_location: Section II, cavity definition before Eq. (3)

technical_objections.evidence: The derivation uses a product/sum over Rindler modes and a trace over the full Hilbert space. A rigorous mathematical version would use a regulator, wave-packet basis, finite volume, or KMS correlation functions.
technical_objections.objection: The thermal density matrix in Eq. (5) is treated as an ordinary object under traces despite continuum modes and infinite products.
technical_objections.paper_location: Section III Eq. (5), Section V Eqs. (10)-(11)

technical_objections.evidence: The Hamiltonian is written with a dxi measure. Depending on whether the model is phenomenological or derived from a covariant action in the Rindler metric, lapse/volume factors or coupling rescalings could change the overlap integrals.
technical_objections.objection: The interaction Hamiltonian in Eq. (6) is not justified relative to a covariant local interaction density or to the exact model used in the 2015 paper.
technical_objections.paper_location: Section IV Eq. (6), Section V Eq. (12)

technical_objections.evidence: The perturbative operator U1 integrates over all Rindler time. Other reviewers correctly noted that this requires an adiabatic prescription or convergence argument, especially because Eq. (11) contains continuum frequency integrals.
technical_objections.objection: The infinite Rindler-time evolution from eta=-infinity to eta=+infinity is used without stating switching, regulator, or convergence assumptions.
technical_objections.paper_location: Section IV Eqs. (7)-(8), Section V Eq. (11)

technical_objections.evidence: The explicit cavity and ambient mode functions are referenced to Eqs. (21)-(23) of [1]. This is acceptable for a Reply, but it leaves normalization and notation matching as a load-bearing external check.
technical_objections.objection: The exact equality between Eq. (11) and Eq. (19) of the 2015 paper is asserted rather than independently verifiable from the Reply alone.
technical_objections.paper_location: Section V, Eqs. (11)-(13) and the sentence after Eq. (13)

technical_objections.evidence: The paper says the evolution within the cavity is independent of how constant-time slices are continued outside it. That would follow from locality/microcausality under suitable assumptions, but these assumptions are not spelled out.
technical_objections.objection: The Section VI foliation-deformation argument is physically plausible but not made precise as a mathematical causality argument.
